clusters.title: Tropical Cyclone Narelle could strengthen into a category 5 before landfall
clusters.neutral_headline: Tropical Cyclone Narelle Approaches Queensland

items.title: Tropical Cyclone Narelle could strengthen into a category 5 before landfall
items.standfirst: Tropical Cyclone Narelle is expected to cross the Queensland coast as a category 5 system, bringing heavy to intense rainfall and strong winds.
items.content: Tropical Cyclone Narelle is expected to strengthen into a category 5 system before it crosses the Queensland coast on Friday morning.The current category 4 system is expected to bring heavy to intense rainfall and strong winds to coastal regions, including communities between Lockhart River and Cape Melville.Bureau of Meteorology senior Meteorologist Christie Johnson said the cyclone reached a category 3 system late on Wednesday afternoon and strengthened further to a category 4 system overnight.LIVE: Ousted security chief's blistering Trump claims"The system is currently located well off the Queensland coast, about 500km east-north-east of Cooktown and will continue to move west today heading for the Queensland coast," Johnson said."A coastal crossing is expected Friday morning."At this stage most likely somewhere between Lockhart River and Cape Melville."The tropical cyclone is forecast to be a category 4 storm as it crosses the coast. "From there, the severe tropical cyclone will continue west while weakening, likely dropping back to a category 2 system by the time it crosses the west coast of the cape yorke peninsula on Friday night," Johnson said."It will then track across the Gulf of Carpenteria on Saturday morning, heading for the Northern Territory and likely intensifying into a severe tropical cyclone."It will then move across the Northern Territory during the weekend."READ MORE: Watchdog puts fuel suppliers in crosshairs Prime Minister Anthony Albanese said the storm had the potential to be a "very dangerous" weather event for Queensland and the Northern Territory."The category 4 cyclone is forecast into a category 5 system before crossing with severe impacts on Friday," Albanese said."I've been engaged and will continue to engage with Premier David Crisafulli."As always the Commonwealth stands ready to provide whatever assistance is required for both Queensland and the Northern Territory."A tropical cyclone warning is current for coastal areas between Lockhart Ri ver and Cape Tribulation as well as adjacent inland parts of the Cape York Peninsula."These areas are likely to see gale force winds and damaging to destructive wind gusts developing during today, with the strongest winds most likely from tonight," Johnson said."As the cyclone crosses the coast we could see peak very destructive winds in excess of 230km/h close to the core of the system, with broader areas of destructive wind gusts up to 160km/h between Lockhart River and Cooktown.READ MORE: PM's blunt message for hoarders ahead of national cabinet meeting on fuel supply "Heavy to intense rainfall will also build in this tropical cyclone warning area through today, more likely from tonight continuing into Friday morning."Flash flooding will become an increasing concern as the cyclone draws near."Ab normally high tides, which may lead to inundation of low-lying areas, are expected to develop along the far north Queensland coast as Narelle approaches and crosses the coast.A d angerous storm tide is forecast around Princess Charlotte Bay, particularly if the system crosses the coast around the time of the high tide on Friday morning.There is a broader tropical cyclone watch area that extends west across the Cape York Peninsula, covering areas between Weipa and Kowanyama on the western or Gulf coast."These areas are likely to see winds and rain increasing, particularly on Friday," Johnson said."We are likely to see the tropical cyclone warning area extending to these areas during the day."As we go into the weekend, the impact from several days of rain will also be felt and riverine flooding is likely across much of Cape York.READ MORE: Government won't rule out fuel rationing as war drags on" Flood watches are in place for the entirety of the Cape York Peninsula with river rises likely over the weekend."Outside of the tropical cyclone warning area, there is also a gale-force wind warning for the Cairns coastal waters today."There is also a chance that severe thunderstorms could develop on the edge of the system and bring localised rainfall and heavy flooding to parts of the north tropical coast, north of about Ingham," Johnson said." If this occurs, then we will issue severe thunderstorm warnings for those thunderstorms."The top end of the Northern Territory is likely to see impacts building over the weekend."At this stage Tropical Cyclone Narelle looks likely to reach the east coast of the Top End late Saturday and into Sunday as a severe tropical cyclone, before moving across southern parts of the Top End Sunday into Monday," Johnson said." We're likely to see the risk of damaging winds, heavy rainfall and flash flooding increasing over eastern parts of the Top End through Saturday as the system approaches and extending westwards on Sunday and Monday."NEVER MISS A STORY: Get your breaking news and exclusive stories first by following us across all platforms.Download the 9NEWS App here via Apple and Google PlayMake 9News your preferred source on Google by ticking this box hereSign up to our breaking newsletter here

clusters.title: ACCC to investigation allegations of anti-competitive conduct by regional fuel retailers
clusters.neutral_headline: ACCC Investigates Fuel Suppliers

items.title: ACCC to investigation allegations of anti-competitive conduct by regional fuel retailers
items.standfirst: The consumer watchdog has announced an investigation into allegations of anti-competitive conduct by major fuel suppliers in regional and rural Australia.
items.content: The consumer watchdog has announced an investigation into allegations of anti-competitive conduct around the supply of diesel by some of Australia's major fuel suppliers in regional and rural Australia.The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission (ACCC) said it has taken the "unusual" step to publicly announce the investigation after it received reports about diesel availability to independent wholesalers and distributors.The watchdog's investigation into the alleged conduct will focus on Ampol, BP Australia, Mobil Oil Australia and Viva Energy Australia.LIVE UPDATES: Trump's claims of a wipeout are blown up in just four words READ MORE: Blunt message for Aussies making fuel shortages worseACCC chair Gina Cass-Gottlieb said the watchdog would closely scrutinise all fuel markets during this period."We have received reports of alleged anti-competitive behaviour," Cass-Gottlieb said."We are therefore investigating these matters urgently."It is not our usual practice to publicly announce investigations, but given the significance of the issue, the ACCC is confirming this enforcement investigation. "We recognise the widespread concerns held by consumers, businesses and farmers about fuel pricing and supply issues arising during the Middle Eastern conflict."It is important that fuel market participants and the community know that we are closely watching market conduct in relation to all fuels and we will not hesitate to act swiftly to enforce Australia's competition and consumer laws."Our investigation is at a preliminary stage, and therefore we are yet to form a view about these matters."READ MORE: Aussie market battered, oil prices surge as Iran and Israel attack major gas hubs NEVER MISS A STORY: Get your breaking news and exclusive stories first by following us across all platforms.Download the 9NEWS App here via Apple and Google PlayMake 9News your preferred source on Google by ticking this box hereSign up to our breaking newsletter here

clusters.title: Red wave expected in SA — but the upper house race is the one to watch
clusters.neutral_headline: Labor Expected To Win In South Australia

items.title: Red wave expected in SA — but the upper house race is the one to watch
items.standfirst: Labor's popularity in South Australia is set to lead to a large number of seats for the party at the upcoming state election, as various right-wing parties compete for a red seat in the upper house, writes ABC Chief Elections and Data Analyst Casey Briggs.
items.content: Labor's popularity in South Australia is set to lead to a large number of seats for the party at the upcoming state election, as various right-wing parties compete for a red seat in the upper house, writes ABC Chief Elections and Data Analyst Casey Briggs.
